tokyoBases.desc: Start with three common Tokyo bases: Shinjuku for energy, Ueno / Asakusa for easier east-side access, and Tokyo Station for Shinkansen and luggage convenience.

shinjuku.name: Shinjuku
shinjuku.bestFor: First-time energy, nightlife, shopping, and flexible train access.
shinjuku.weakness: Big stations and late-night crowds can feel intense on arrival day. Never follow street touts in Kabukicho — overcharging and scam bars are a real risk.

shibuya.name: Shibuya
shibuya.bestFor: Fashion, food, nightlife, youth culture, and easy west-side Tokyo access.
shibuya.weakness: Crowded, hilly around some hotels, and not as simple for early Shinkansen days.

ueno.name: Ueno
ueno.bestFor: Budget hotels, Narita access, museums, and market food.
ueno.weakness: Less late-night variety than Shinjuku or Shibuya.

asakusa.name: Asakusa
asakusa.bestFor: Traditional Tokyo, Senso-ji, riverside walks, and old-town texture.
asakusa.weakness: Not on the JR Yamanote Line, so cross-city trips need transfers.

tokyoStation.name: Tokyo Station
tokyoStation.bestFor: Early Shinkansen, clean logistics, Ginza access, and efficient travel days.
tokyoStation.weakness: More businesslike and usually pricier than Ueno or Asakusa.

eastTokyo.name: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa / East Tokyo
eastTokyo.bestFor: Quiet local Tokyo, coffee, gardens, museums, and slower mornings.
eastTokyo.weakness: Better as a calm detour or second-time base than a default first-night choice.

uenoAsakusa.name: Ueno / Asakusa
uenoAsakusa.bestFor: Narita access, budget stays, and old-town Tokyo
uenoAsakusa.weakness: less nightlife than Shinjuku or Shibuya

localTokyo.eyebrow: Local Tokyo picks from a Japanese local
localTokyo.desc: A Local Lens for quieter east-side neighborhoods beyond Shinjuku and Shibuya, including Oshiage for Skytree views and riverside walks.

kiyosumi.name: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa
kiyosumi.summary: Coffee, Kiyosumi Garden, Fukagawa Edo Museum, art museums, and slow canal-side walks. Best when you want a calm east-side morning rather than another busy sightseeing stop.
kiyosumi.avoidIf: you want nightlife or first-night simplicity

kuramae.name: Kuramae
kuramae.summary: Design shops, small makers, riverside cafes, and a softer Asakusa pairing.
kuramae.avoidIf: you need major sights in one stop

oshiage.name: Oshiage
oshiage.summary: Skytree views, Sumida riverside walks, and calmer east-side stays. Next-door Kinshicho has a spacious Daiso, Muji, and gashapon floor without Shibuya crowds.
oshiage.avoidIf: you want nightlife or a central feel

monzenNakacho.name: Monzen-Nakacho
monzenNakacho.summary: Old Tokyo streets, shrines, neighborhood izakaya, and easy East Tokyo add-ons.

ryogoku.name: Ryogoku
ryogoku.summary: Sumo culture, Edo history, museums, and a useful bridge toward Asakusa.

suitengumae.name: Suitengumae / Ningyocho
suitengumae.summary: T-CAT airport bus access, quiet hotels, Ningyocho food streets, and an easy bridge between Tokyo Station and East Tokyo.
suitengumae.bestFor: airport transfer, calm hotels, old-town food
suitengumae.avoidIf: you want nightlife or headline sights

guide.h1: How to see Mt. Fuji from the Shinkansen (Tokyo ⇄ Osaka/Kyoto)
guide.subtitle: A practical guide to the best Shinkansen seats, sections, and timing for Mt. Fuji views.
guide.writtenBy: Written by a Japanese local, for visitors who don't want to miss the Mt. Fuji × Shinkansen view.
guide.introH2: Mt. Fuji view basics on the Tokaido Shinkansen
guide.introP1: Many visitors to Japan want to see Mt. Fuji from the Shinkansen, but it's easy to miss if you don't know which side or section to sit in. This guide explains where and when Mt. Fuji is visible from the Tokaido Shinkansen between Tokyo and Osaka/Kyoto, and how to make it easier by using our free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker tool.
guide.introP1Bold: where and when Mt. Fuji is visible from the Tokaido Shinkansen
guide.introP2: You won't see Mt. Fuji for the whole ride – it appears only for a short stretch of the journey, and it can be completely hidden by clouds on some days.

tldr.text: Tokyo → Osaka/Kyoto = right side. Osaka/Kyoto → Tokyo = left side.
In most standard 3+2 cars, Seat E is the Mt. Fuji window seat.
Around Shin-Fuji station, between Shin-Yokohama and Shizuoka.
Clear days, usually late morning to early afternoon (season-dependent).
Use the free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker on fujiseat.com before you book.

guide.s1H2: Which side of the Shinkansen is Mt. Fuji on? (Seat E explained)
guide.s1Bullet1: Mt. Fuji is on the right side of the train (window seat E in most standard cars).
guide.s1Bullet2: Mt. Fuji is on the left side of the train (again, window seat E in most standard cars).
guide.s1Note: In most standard (non–Green Car) 3+2 seat cars, Seat E is the window seat on the Mt. Fuji side. Green Cars may have a different layout, but the same left/right rule still applies. Some older guides still mention different seat letters, but on most modern 3+2 standard cars, Seat E is the Mt. Fuji window seat.
guide.s2H2: Best section and timing to see Mt. Fuji from the train
guide.s2P1: You won't see Mt. Fuji for the whole ride – it appears only for a short stretch of the journey. Mt. Fuji is usually visible on clear days between Shin-Yokohama and Shizuoka, especially around Shin-Fuji station.
guide.s2Bullet1: Tokaido Shinkansen between Tokyo, Nagoya and Osaka/Kyoto (Nozomi / Hikari / Kodama).
guide.s2Bullet2: Late morning to early afternoon often gives a clearer silhouette. In summer, early morning can also work well.
guide.s2Bullet3: On cloudy or hazy days, Mt. Fuji may be hard to see even from the correct side and seat.
guide.s3H2: Practical seat tips for a better Mt. Fuji view
guide.s3Bullet1: When booking, choose a reserved seat in a standard car and request seat E on the Mt. Fuji side if possible.
guide.s3Bullet2: Window seats near the middle of the car often have the clearest views, away from doors and bulkheads.
guide.s3Bullet3: Mt. Fuji can appear and disappear quickly, especially around Shin-Fuji station.
guide.s3Bullet4: Some trains announce when Mt. Fuji is visible—listen for English announcements if you're not watching the map.
guide.s4H2: Common mistakes when trying to see Mt. Fuji
guide.s4Bullet1: Forgetting that Tokyo → Osaka/Kyoto is right side, and the opposite for the return.
guide.s4Bullet2: Even with the correct seat, clouds or haze can fully cover the mountain.
guide.s4Bullet3: If you start looking only near Shizuoka, you might miss the best view around Shin-Fuji.
guide.jrH2: JR Pass vs Single Ticket: Which is cheaper?
guide.jrPassWorth: JR Pass is usually worth comparing only if you take multiple long Shinkansen rides (e.g. Tokyo → Kyoto → Hiroshima → Tokyo). For a simple Tokyo ↔ Osaka round trip, single tickets are often cheaper.
guide.jrNozomiNote: Note: The Nozomi (fastest) may require an additional NOZOMI MIZUHO Ticket (approx. ¥4,960 Tokyo→Kyoto/Osaka) on top of your JR Pass. Without it, use Hikari or Kodama — these stop near Shin-Fuji, which is actually better for Mt. Fuji views anyway.
guide.seasonH2: Best Season & Time of Day to See Mt. Fuji

seasons.note: Clear skies, early snow from October

timeItems.text: Sun glare possible on the Mt. Fuji side

guide.proTip: Pro tip: Check Shizuoka Prefecture weather the night before. Our tool shows live visibility near Shin-Fuji at the top of the page.
carStandard: Cars 4–7 often give the clearest unobstructed views
Seat E = Mt. Fuji window seat in all standard cars
carGreen: Mt. Fuji window seat = Seat D

carRequest.text: select column E on the seat map
reserve free at any JR station counter

faq.q: Can I see Mt. Fuji from a non-reserved car?
faq.a: Yes, but risky — you may end up in an aisle seat with no view. Reserve Seat E in advance.

faq.q: How long can I see Mt. Fuji from the train?
faq.a: Only about 30–60 seconds at Shinkansen speed. Have your camera ready before reaching Shin-Fuji station.

faq.q: Is Seat E always the Mt. Fuji side?
faq.a: In standard 3+2 cars, yes. In Green Cars (2+2 layout), the Mt. Fuji window seat is Seat D.
Mt. Fuji is often hidden, especially in summer. Check our live visibility indicator at the top of the page.

faq.q: Can I see Mt. Fuji on the return trip (Osaka/Kyoto → Tokyo)?
faq.a: Yes — Mt. Fuji is on the LEFT side, which is again Seat E. Use our checker and select the opposite direction.

faq.q: Does the Nozomi stop near Mt. Fuji?
faq.a: No, but you can still see it through the window as the train passes. Hikari and Kodama stop at Shin-Fuji station.

faq.q: Is the JR Pass worth it for Tokyo–Osaka only?
faq.a: Generally no. Round trip ≈ ¥29,000 vs 7-day Pass ¥50,000. The Pass makes sense if also visiting Hiroshima, Nara, etc.

faq.q: Can I bring large luggage on the Shinkansen?
faq.a: Bags with total dimensions over 160cm and up to 250cm require a seat reservation with an oversized baggage area (予約が必要). Reserve this when booking your Shinkansen seat. Bags over 250cm are not permitted.

faq.q: Is there WiFi on the Shinkansen?
faq.a: Onboard WiFi exists but can be unreliable. A Japan eSIM is recommended for consistent connectivity throughout your trip.

faq.q: What is the best way to book as a foreigner?
faq.a: Klook — fully in English, instant mobile voucher, and you can select Seat E on the seat map.

guide.makeEasyH2: Make it easy with the Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker
guide.makeEasyP1: If you don't want to remember all the details, you can use our free Shinkansen Mt. Fuji Seat Checker. Just choose your direction and it tells you which seat to book for the best chance of seeing Mt. Fuji.
guide.makeEasyP2: The tool is designed to work well on mobile, so you can quickly check it while booking tickets or travelling in Japan.
guide.commandCenterP: Got your Shinkansen seat sorted? Plan the rest of the trip with route templates, booking essentials, weather, currency and a pre-departure checklist.
guide.priorityH2: Priority Seats — What Foreigners Should Know
guide.priorityP1: You'll notice seats marked 優先席 (yūsen-seki) near the doors of most Japanese trains. These are priority seats reserved for elderly passengers, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those carrying small children.
guide.priorityP2: While not legally prohibited, sitting in priority seats when you don't need them is considered poor manners in Japan. Many Japanese people avoid these seats entirely — even when the train is not crowded — because occupying them carries an unspoken social stigma.
guide.priorityP3: As a visitor, the safest approach is to simply avoid priority seats altogether. If the train is packed and no one who needs the seat is nearby, sitting briefly is understandable — but be ready to stand immediately when someone who needs it boards.
guide.priorityTip: Look for the stickers on windows and the different-colored seat fabric (usually grey or patterned) to identify priority seats. When in doubt, choose a regular seat.
guide.devP: I'm also building a small side project called KanjiFlick — a swipe-style list of single kanji meanings (the kind Japanese kids learn). It's not a travel app, but if you're curious about the characters you'll see around Japan, you might enjoy it.
guide.priorityShortNoteBody: priority seats (優先席) are different from Shinkansen reserved seats. If you are unsure, avoid them or offer the seat immediately.
guide.priorityShortNoteLink: Read basic Japanese train signs and etiquette

kuramae.title: Kuramae Guide — Design Shops, Cafes and Makers in East Tokyo
kuramae.description: A practical local guide to Kuramae, Tokyo's quiet design and maker neighborhood. Best for craft shops, riverside cafes, and travelers who want a creative, walkable side of east Tokyo.
kuramae.whyVisit: Kuramae is where Tokyo's design and craft scene lives without the tourist markup. Old warehouses turned into leather studios, glass workshops, and single-origin roasters line quiet streets between the Sumida River and Asakusa. It rewards slow browsing: walk into a shop, talk to the maker, and buy something you cannot find in Shibuya.
route: Coffee at a Kuramae roaster near the station
Browse design shops along Kuramae-dori and side streets
Walk along the Sumida River toward Asakusa
Lunch at a local cafe or continue to Asakusa for traditional food
Optional: add Kiyosumi-Shirakawa for gardens and museums
kuramae.shouldStay: Good for repeat visitors or travelers who value design over sightseeing density. First-timers usually do better near Asakusa or Ueno. Kuramae is one station away and easy as a morning add-on without sleeping there. If you want a creative, quiet base and do not mind being a short ride from major sights, it works well.

monzen-nakacho.title: Monzen-Nakacho Guide — Shrines, Izakaya and Old East Tokyo
monzen-nakacho.description: A practical local guide to Monzen-Nakacho, Tokyo's old-town neighborhood with shrines, izakaya streets, and local food. Best for afternoon walks, traditional atmosphere, and travelers who want a slower east-side evening.
monzen-nakacho.whyVisit: Monzen-Nakacho is one of the few Tokyo neighborhoods where the old shitamachi atmosphere still holds. Tomioka Hachimangu shrine anchors the area, and the surrounding streets are lined with small izakaya, local lunch spots, and traditional shops that cater to regulars, not tourists. It gives travelers a taste of Tokyo's east-side character without the curated feel of Yanaka or Asakusa.
route: Start from Kiyosumi-Shirakawa or arrive at Monzen-Nakacho Station
Visit Tomioka Hachimangu shrine
Walk the backstreets for local shops and small eateries
Early evening izakaya along Eitai-dori or side alleys
Optional: continue to Tsukiji or Tokyo Station for dinner
monzen-nakacho.shouldStay: Monzen-Nakacho works as a local base for travelers who already know their way around Tokyo and want a quieter, more residential feel. For first-timers, it is better as a half-day add-on paired with Kiyosumi-Shirakawa in the morning. The Tozai line gives easy access to Nihonbashi and Otemachi for business travelers.

oshiage.title: Oshiage Guide — Skytree, Local Streets and a Quieter Asakusa Alternative
oshiage.description: A practical local guide to Oshiage, the Tokyo Skytree neighborhood. Best for Skytree views, Solamachi shopping, and travelers who want a calmer alternative to Asakusa with easy airport access.
oshiage.bestTiming: Late afternoon into evening for Skytree lights. Morning for quiet streets.
oshiage.whyVisit: Oshiage gives travelers the Tokyo Skytree experience plus a surprisingly quiet residential neighborhood one block away. The Solamachi mall at the base of Skytree covers souvenirs, food, and a planetarium. Beyond the tower, Oshiage's backstreets feel like old shitamachi Tokyo: small shops, local eateries, and none of the Asakusa crowds. The Tobu Skytree line and Hanzomon metro make Narita and Haneda access straightforward.

extra.title: Kinshicho — the next station over
extra.body: One stop from Oshiage on the Hanzomon line, Kinshicho has no headline sights but is quietly useful for shopping. The large commercial complex next to the station houses a spacious Daiso, Muji, and a big gashapon capsule toy floor, all far less crowded than their Shibuya or Shinjuku equivalents. Don Quijote is also nearby.

route: Arrive at Oshiage Station and walk to Tokyo Skytree
Browse Solamachi for souvenirs or lunch
Walk the quiet backstreets east of the tower
Cross toward Asakusa along the Sumida River
Optional: one stop to Kinshicho for Daiso, Muji, and gashapon shopping
Optional: Skytree observation deck in the late afternoon for sunset views
oshiage.shouldStay: Oshiage is a practical budget alternative to Asakusa. Hotels are cheaper, airport access is direct via Tobu Skytree line and Keisei line, and Asakusa is a 20-minute walk or one stop away. The trade-off: fewer restaurants and less evening atmosphere than Asakusa. It works well for a first or last night when airport logistics matter, or as a budget base for travelers who do not mind a quieter neighborhood.

ryogoku.title: Ryogoku Guide — Sumo, Edo History and Museums in East Tokyo
ryogoku.description: A practical local guide to Ryogoku, Tokyo's sumo and Edo history district. Best for the Edo-Tokyo Museum, sumo culture, and travelers who want a historical side of east Tokyo.
ryogoku.whyVisit: Ryogoku is where Tokyo's sumo tradition and Edo-period history live side by side. The Kokugikan sumo arena hosts major tournaments three times a year, and even outside tournament season the neighborhood is full of sumo stables, chanko nabe restaurants, and the Edo-Tokyo Museum. It is a focused, half-day destination that pairs naturally with Asakusa across the river.
route: Arrive at Ryogoku Station on the JR Sobu line
Edo-Tokyo Museum for a thorough Edo and Meiji overview
Walk past Kokugikan and look for sumo wrestlers near stables
Chanko nabe lunch at a local restaurant
Cross the Sumida River to Asakusa or continue to Kiyosumi-Shirakawa
ryogoku.shouldStay: Ryogoku is best as a half-day visit rather than a base. Hotel options are limited compared to Asakusa or Ueno, and evening dining is quieter than most travelers prefer. Visit for the museum, sumo, and chanko, then continue to a busier area. If you want to stay nearby, Asakusa is across the river and offers more options.

suitengumae-ningyocho.title: Suitengumae / Ningyocho Guide — T-CAT, Hotels and Old Tokyo Food Streets
suitengumae-ningyocho.description: A practical local guide to Suitengumae and Ningyocho, Tokyo's airport bus hub with calm hotels, old-town food streets and easy access to Nihonbashi, Tokyo Station and East Tokyo.
suitengumae-ningyocho.bestTiming: Arrival day, departure eve, or a light half-day.
suitengumae-ningyocho.combineWith: Ningyocho, Nihonbashi, Kiyosumi-Shirakawa, or Tokyo Station.
suitengumae-ningyocho.whyVisit: Suitengumae is not a flashy sightseeing stop, and that is exactly why it is useful. The Tokyo City Air Terminal makes airport bus transfers simple, hotels are calmer than Shinjuku or Shibuya, and Ningyocho is close enough for old-town snacks, small restaurants, and a more local first evening. It works as a practical East Tokyo hub rather than a destination you force into the itinerary.
route: Arrive via T-CAT or Suitengumae Station
Walk toward Ningyocho for old-town food streets
Stop by Amazake Yokocho or small local snack shops
Continue to Nihonbashi or Tokyo Station if you need city logistics
Optional: add Kiyosumi-Shirakawa for coffee, gardens, and museums
suitengumae-ningyocho.shouldStay: Stay here if airport transfer, quiet hotels, and easy movement matter more than nightlife. It is especially useful for a first night after a long flight, a final night before an airport bus, or travelers who want to be near Tokyo Station without paying Tokyo Station prices. If you want big sights at your door, Asakusa, Ueno, or Shinjuku will feel clearer.

benefits.title: Why pick your area before booking?

items.title: Time saved
items.body: Better access to your airport and trains.

items.title: Better value
items.body: Each area has different price and vibe.

items.title: Better experience
items.body: Your base shapes your whole Tokyo trip.

items.question: Is Shinjuku too noisy for families?
items.answer: It can be around Kabukicho. Families usually do better around Nishi-Shinjuku, Yoyogi, or Shinjuku-Gyoenmae.

items.question: Is Ueno good for first-time visitors?
items.answer: Yes, especially if you arrive at Narita or want better-value hotels with museums and markets nearby.

items.question: Should I stay near Tokyo Station before Kyoto or Osaka?
items.answer: Yes if your Shinkansen is early or you have heavy luggage. Otherwise Shinjuku or Ueno can still work well.

items.question: Is Asakusa convenient without the JR Yamanote Line?
items.answer: It is convenient for old-town sightseeing, but you will use subway transfers more often than in Ueno or Shinjuku.

items.question: Where should I stay if I arrive late at Narita?
items.answer: Ueno is usually the simplest because the Skyliner is fast and the area is calmer on arrival night.

items.question: Is East Tokyo good for a first Tokyo hotel?
items.answer: It can be, but it is better for travelers who already know they want a quieter local base.

shinjuku.title: Where to Stay in Shinjuku
shinjuku.subtitle: Best for first-time energy, food, nightlife, and train options — but choose the calmer side if you want quiet nights.
shinjuku.bestFor: First-timers who want food, nightlife, shopping, and flexible train access in one base.
shinjuku.avoid: Light sleepers who book directly inside the loudest nightlife blocks without checking the micro-area.
shinjuku.bestMicroAreas: Nishi-Shinjuku, Shinjuku-Gyoenmae / Shinjuku-sanchome, and Yoyogi / South Shinjuku.

microAreas.name: Nishi-Shinjuku
microAreas.body: Business hotels, quieter nights, good for families and comfort.

microAreas.name: Shinjuku-Gyoenmae / Shinjuku-sanchome
microAreas.body: Still central, calmer, good food access.

microAreas.name: Kabukicho
microAreas.body: Bright, loud, convenient, but not ideal for quiet stays.

faqs.question: Is Shinjuku too noisy?
faqs.answer: Some blocks are. Stay west, south, or near Shinjuku-Gyoenmae if quiet matters.

faqs.question: Is Shinjuku good for families?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially Nishi-Shinjuku or Yoyogi / South Shinjuku.

faqs.question: Is Kabukicho a bad place to stay?
faqs.answer: Not automatically, but it is loud and nightlife-heavy. It is not the calmest first-night choice.

faqs.question: How far is Shinjuku from Tokyo Station?
faqs.answer: Usually 10-15 minutes by JR Chuo Line, plus walking time inside stations.

ueno.title: Where to Stay in Ueno
ueno.subtitle: Best for Narita access, museums, parks, and better-value hotels.
ueno.bestFor: Travelers arriving at Narita, museum days, Ueno Park, and practical central Tokyo value.
ueno.avoid: Travelers who want late-night nightlife at their hotel doorstep.
ueno.bestMicroAreas: Ueno Station / Okachimachi, Ueno Park side, Inaricho / Taito side, and Ameyoko side.

faqs.question: Is Ueno good for first-time visitors?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially if Narita access and value matter more than nightlife.

faqs.question: Is Ueno convenient for the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: Tokyo Station is about 7 minutes away by JR, and some northern Shinkansen services also stop at Ueno.

faqs.question: Is Ueno lively at night?
faqs.answer: It has food and market energy, but it is much quieter than Shinjuku.

faqs.question: Where should I stay near Ueno?
faqs.answer: Station / Okachimachi is easiest; Inaricho can feel calmer and better value.

asakusa.title: Where to Stay in Asakusa
asakusa.subtitle: Best for old-town Tokyo, Senso-ji, riverside walks, and a traditional atmosphere.
asakusa.bestFor: Travelers who want temples, old-town atmosphere, Tokyo Skytree views, and calmer evenings.
asakusa.avoid: Travelers who want JR Yamanote access or late-night west-side nightlife every night.
asakusa.bestMicroAreas: Senso-ji / Kaminarimon side, Tawaramachi side, Sumida River side, and Kuramae edge.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa convenient without JR?
faqs.answer: Yes for east Tokyo and old-town sights, but you will use subway transfers more often.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa good for families?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is calmer than Shinjuku and has easy temple and river walks.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa too quiet at night?
faqs.answer: It quiets down earlier than Shinjuku, which can be a benefit if you want calm nights.

faqs.question: Where is the best Asakusa micro-area?
faqs.answer: Kaminarimon is easiest for first-timers; Tawaramachi and Kuramae edge are calmer.

tokyo-station.title: Where to Stay near Tokyo Station
tokyo-station.subtitle: Best for early Shinkansen, luggage, business-like comfort, and easy Kyoto / Osaka transfers.
tokyo-station.bestFor: Early Shinkansen departures, heavy luggage, Kyoto / Osaka transfers, and clean logistics.
tokyo-station.avoid: Travelers who want late-night local neighborhood energy right outside the hotel.
tokyo-station.bestMicroAreas: Marunouchi side, Yaesu side, Nihonbashi / Kyobashi side, and Ginza edge.

faqs.question: Should I stay near Tokyo Station before Kyoto?
faqs.answer: Yes if your train is early or luggage is heavy. It removes the morning transfer.

faqs.question: Is Tokyo Station too business-like?
faqs.answer: It can feel less local at night, but it is excellent for logistics.

faqs.question: Which side is best for the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: Yaesu is usually the practical side for Shinkansen and bus access.

faqs.question: Is Ginza close enough?
faqs.answer: Yes for many travelers, especially if shopping and restaurants matter.

east-tokyo.title: Where to Stay in East Tokyo
east-tokyo.subtitle: Best for quieter local walks, coffee, riverside neighborhoods, and second-time Tokyo visitors.
east-tokyo.bestFor: Travelers who want slower streets, coffee, museums, riverside walks, and a quieter base.
east-tokyo.avoid: First-timers who want the simplest default base for every major Tokyo sight.

faqs.question: Is East Tokyo good for a first Tokyo hotel?
faqs.answer: It can be, but it is better if you already know you want a quieter local base.

faqs.question: Which East Tokyo area is best for coffee?
faqs.answer: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa is the strongest coffee and museum base.

faqs.question: Is Kuramae convenient?
faqs.answer: It is good for Asakusa and riverside walks, but less direct for west-side nightlife.

faqs.question: Should I stay in Ryogoku?
faqs.answer: It works for sumo, museums, and local food, but it is usually better as a focused local base than a default first stay.

hero.title: Where to Stay in Kyoto for First-Time Visitors
hero.subtitle: Pick the right Kyoto base before you book. Kyoto Station solves luggage and day trips; Gion and Higashiyama solve atmosphere; Kawaramachi / Shijo keeps food and shopping central.

quickAnswer: Choose Kyoto Station if transport, luggage, and day trips matter most.
Choose Kawaramachi / Shijo if you want food, shopping, and central convenience.
Choose Gion / Higashiyama if temples and traditional Kyoto atmosphere matter most.
Choose Arashiyama for a scenic stay, but expect a less central base.
Choose Kyoto Imperial Palace / quieter north if you want calmer streets on a repeat visit.

hero.title: Where to Stay in Osaka for First-Time Visitors
hero.subtitle: Pick the Osaka base by how you will use the city. Namba is easiest for food and nightlife; Umeda is stronger for rail; Shin-Osaka is a logistics choice.

quickAnswer: Choose Namba if food, nightlife, and first-time Osaka energy matter most.
Choose Umeda if rail access, Kyoto / Kobe trips, and airport connections matter most.
Choose Shin-Osaka if Shinkansen logistics are the main priority.
Choose Tennoji if you want value and south Osaka access.
Choose Osaka Castle / quieter central if you want a calmer base.

tokyo-first-time.title: Where to Stay in Tokyo for First-Time Visitors — Best Hotel Areas by Trip Style
tokyo-first-time.description: Compare Shinjuku, Ueno, Asakusa, Tokyo Station, Ginza and quieter East Tokyo before booking hotels. Choose by airport access, luggage, Shinkansen plans, nightlife and first-night comfort.

quickRec.why: Best balance of nightlife, transport access, and hotel density. 10 min to Tokyo Station for Shinkansen day trips. Most first-timers start here.

tokyo-first-time.proTip: If you're taking the Shinkansen to Kyoto on Day 3+, staying in Shinjuku and transferring to Tokyo Station is more fun than staying near Tokyo Station the whole time.

faqs.question: Where should a first-time visitor stay in Tokyo?
faqs.answer: Shinjuku is the best all-round base — strong transport, lots of hotels, and about 10 minutes to Tokyo Station for Shinkansen day trips. Choose Ueno for value and Narita access, Asakusa for atmosphere, or Tokyo Station if you have an early Shinkansen.

faqs.question: Which Tokyo area is easiest with luggage after a long flight?
faqs.answer: Ueno (direct Keisei Skyliner from Narita) or Tokyo Station are the easiest first-night bases. If you are jet-lagged, the huge Shinjuku station can be tiring on the first night — it is a better base once you are oriented.

faqs.question: How many nights should I stay in Tokyo?
faqs.answer: Most first-timers spend 3 to 4 nights in Tokyo, based in one area, and day-trip from there. Pick a single well-connected base rather than moving hotels every night, which wastes time with luggage.

shinjuku-vs-ueno-vs-asakusa.title: Shinjuku vs Ueno vs Asakusa — which Tokyo base is right?
shinjuku-vs-ueno-vs-asakusa.description: Shinjuku vs Ueno vs Asakusa for your Tokyo hotel: Shinjuku for transport and nightlife, Ueno for value and Narita access (Skyliner 36 min), Asakusa for old-town atmosphere and Senso-ji. A head-to-head with hotel picks for first-time visitors.

quickRec.why: If you can only pick one, Shinjuku wins on transport flexibility, dining variety, and nightlife. Ueno is better for value, while Asakusa is stronger for atmosphere.

shinjuku-vs-ueno-vs-asakusa.proTip: For a short split stay, use Shinjuku for nightlife and shopping, then Asakusa for a calmer temple morning.

faqs.question: Which of Shinjuku, Ueno, or Asakusa is best for first-time Tokyo?
faqs.answer: Shinjuku is the best all-rounder for transport flexibility, dining, and hotel choice. Ueno is better for value and Narita access, and Asakusa is stronger for old-town atmosphere. Most first-timers do well starting in Shinjuku unless budget or airport convenience is the priority.

faqs.question: Which of the three is cheapest for hotels?
faqs.answer: Ueno and Asakusa are generally cheaper than Shinjuku, and Ueno has the most budget hotels and hostels. Shinjuku's central rooms tend to cost more, though it also has the widest overall range. Compare live prices before booking.

faqs.question: Which is easiest from Narita Airport?
faqs.answer: Ueno — the Keisei Skyliner reaches it directly in 36 minutes. Asakusa is about 55 minutes via the Access Express, and Shinjuku takes longer (N'EX roughly 80 minutes or a limousine bus). For a smooth first night after a long flight, Ueno is simplest.

kyoto-station-vs-gion.proTip: A practical split is one night near Kyoto Station for arrival, then Gion or Kawaramachi for the rest after forwarding luggage.

faqs.question: Is Kyoto Station a good place to stay?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is one of the most practical bases for first-time Kyoto because Shinkansen, buses, lockers, and day-trip trains are all close.

faqs.question: Is Gion better than Kyoto Station?
faqs.answer: Gion is better for atmosphere and evening walks. Kyoto Station is better for luggage, Shinkansen access, and day trips.

faqs.question: Where should I stay in Kyoto with luggage?
faqs.answer: Stay near Kyoto Station if luggage is the main concern. Narrow streets around Gion make rolling bags harder.

faqs.question: Is Kyoto Station better for day trips?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nara, Osaka, Fushimi Inari, and the main bus terminal are easiest from Kyoto Station.

faqs.question: Should I stay near Tokyo Station for the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: Yes if you have an early Shinkansen or are travelling luggage-first — Tokyo Station has every Shinkansen line with zero transfer, so departure mornings are effortless. If your train is later or mid-trip, Shinjuku is fine: it is about 10 minutes away by JR Chuo Line and has far more to do.

faqs.question: Which is cheaper, Tokyo Station or Shinjuku hotels?
faqs.answer: Shinjuku usually offers a wider price range, including mid-range and budget rooms. The Tokyo Station, Marunouchi, and Ginza side skews toward business and upscale hotels, so nightly rates tend to be higher. Check live prices for both before booking.

faqs.question: Is Shinjuku or Tokyo Station better for the airport?
faqs.answer: Both have the Narita Express (N'EX) running direct, so Narita is easy from either. Shinjuku also has frequent limousine buses straight to many hotels, which is handy with luggage. For Haneda, both connect simply via train or bus.

ueno-vs-shinjuku.title: Ueno vs Shinjuku: Which Tokyo Area Should You Stay In?
ueno-vs-shinjuku.description: Ueno or Shinjuku for your Tokyo hotel? Ueno wins on Narita access (Keisei Skyliner 36 min) and budget rooms; Shinjuku wins on nightlife, dining density, and transport flexibility. The two are 25 min apart on the JR Yamanote Line. Compared with hotel picks.

quickRec.why: Ueno is smarter for Narita arrivals and value. Shinjuku is stronger for nightlife, food density, and first-time Tokyo energy.

ueno-vs-shinjuku.proTip: Late Narita arrival? Use Ueno for the first night, then move to Shinjuku if you want more nightlife later.

pros: More budget hotel options in central Tokyo
Museums, park, and Ameyoko market nearby
areas.transport: Skyliner to Narita 36 min. JR Yamanote to Shinjuku 25 min. Metro to Asakusa 5 min.

pros: Best nightlife, food, and shopping in one area
Golden Gai and Kabukicho walking distance
areas.transport: 10 min to Tokyo Station. Narita Express direct. Odakyu to Hakone.

faqs.question: Ueno or Shinjuku for Narita Airport?
faqs.answer: Ueno — the Keisei Skyliner runs direct from Narita to Ueno in 36 minutes, cheaper and with fewer transfers. Shinjuku from Narita takes longer (N'EX about 80 minutes, or a limousine bus), so Ueno is the easier first-night base for Narita arrivals.

faqs.question: Which is cheaper for hotels, Ueno or Shinjuku?
faqs.answer: Ueno. It has more budget hotels and hostels, especially around the station and Okachimachi. Shinjuku has a wider overall range but central rooms generally cost more. Check live prices for both.

faqs.question: Is Shinjuku worth it over Ueno for first-timers?
faqs.answer: If you want nightlife, big-city energy, and the most transport options, Shinjuku is worth it. If you prioritise value, Narita access, and museums, Ueno is smarter. They are only 25 minutes apart on the JR Yamanote Line, so you can easily visit both.

faqs.question: Is Namba or Umeda better for first-time Osaka?
faqs.answer: Namba is usually better for classic Osaka food and nightlife. Umeda is better for rail access and Kyoto or Kobe day trips.

faqs.question: Which is better for nightlife, Namba or Umeda?
faqs.answer: Namba. Dotonbori, Amerikamura, and Shinsekai are easier on foot from Namba.

faqs.question: Which area is easier from Kansai Airport?
faqs.answer: Namba is easier because the Nankai line runs directly from KIX to Namba.

faqs.question: Should I stay in Namba if visiting Dotonbori?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dotonbori is in the Namba area, so you can walk there easily.

shin-osaka-vs-namba.title: Shin-Osaka vs Namba: Where Should You Stay in Osaka?
shin-osaka-vs-namba.description: Compare Shin-Osaka and Namba for Shinkansen access, nightlife, food, luggage, early trains, and first-time Osaka trips.

quickRec.why: Choose Namba for most sightseeing stays. Choose Shin-Osaka only when Shinkansen logistics are the main priority.

shin-osaka-vs-namba.proTip: Shin-Osaka saves time for late arrivals or early departures. For everything else, Namba feels much more like Osaka.

faqs.question: Is Shin-Osaka a good place to stay?
faqs.answer: It is practical for early Shinkansen departures or late arrivals, but it is not the best area for food, nightlife, or sightseeing.

faqs.question: Is Namba better than Shin-Osaka for first-time visitors?
faqs.answer: Yes for most sightseeing trips. Namba puts you close to Dotonbori, street food, and nightlife.

faqs.question: Should I stay near Shin-Osaka for the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: Only if your train is very early, you arrive late, or luggage logistics matter more than atmosphere.

faqs.question: Is Namba convenient for Kansai Airport?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Nankai line connects Namba and Kansai Airport directly.

tokyo-station-hotels-before-shinkansen.proTip: If the train leaves before 8am, stay near Tokyo Station or Ueno. If it leaves after 10am, most central areas work.

faqs.question: Should I stay near Tokyo Station before the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: Yes if your train is early or luggage is heavy. For later departures, Shinjuku or Ueno can also work.

faqs.question: Is Shinjuku too far from Tokyo Station for the Shinkansen?
faqs.answer: No. JR Chuo Line connects Shinjuku and Tokyo Station quickly, but rush hour and luggage can make it less comfortable.

faqs.question: Which area has more budget options near Tokyo Station?
faqs.answer: Ueno usually has more budget options while still staying close to Tokyo Station by JR.

faqs.question: Can I store luggage at Tokyo Station?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tokyo Station has coin lockers, staffed storage, and luggage forwarding options.

asakusa-vs-ueno.title: Asakusa vs Ueno: Where Should You Stay in Tokyo?
asakusa-vs-ueno.description: Asakusa or Ueno for your Tokyo hotel? Ueno wins on Narita access (Skyliner 36 min), budget hotels, and a 7-min hop to Tokyo Station; Asakusa wins on old-town atmosphere and Senso-ji. Side-by-side compare with hotel picks for first-time visitors.

quickRec.why: Ueno is usually the more practical first-time base thanks to Narita access and JR links. Asakusa is better for atmosphere and temple walks.

asakusa-vs-ueno.proTip: Ueno and Asakusa are very close, so you can stay in the practical one and visit the atmospheric one easily.

areas.vibe: Traditional Tokyo — temples, Skytree, calm evenings
areas.transport: Metro to Ueno 5 min. Access Express to Narita about 55 min.

pros: More budget hotel options in central Tokyo
Museums, park, and Ameyoko market nearby
areas.transport: Skyliner to Narita 36 min. JR Yamanote to Shinjuku 25 min. Metro to Asakusa 5 min.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa or Ueno better for first-time Tokyo?
faqs.answer: Ueno is more practical for Narita and JR access. Asakusa is better for old-town atmosphere.

faqs.question: Which is closer to Narita Airport?
faqs.answer: Ueno is faster because the Skyliner runs directly from Narita to Ueno.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa good for families?
faqs.answer: Yes. Senso-ji, Nakamise, and Skytree are family-friendly, and evenings are calmer than Shinjuku.

faqs.question: Can I walk from Ueno to Asakusa?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is about a 20-minute walk, or one short ride on the Ginza Line.

faqs.question: Is Asakusa or Ueno cheaper for hotels?
faqs.answer: Ueno generally has more budget hotels and hostels, especially around Ueno Station and Okachimachi, so it is the easier area to find a low-priced room. Asakusa has good value too but fewer rooms overall, so prices can rise on busy dates. Check live prices for both before deciding.

faqs.question: Ueno or Asakusa for an easy first night with luggage?
faqs.answer: Ueno is easier: the Keisei Skyliner runs direct from Narita (36 min) straight to Ueno, so you arrive with minimal transfers. From Asakusa the Access Express takes about 55 minutes. If your first night is right after a long flight, Ueno keeps luggage handling simplest.

faqs.question: Which has more to do at night, Asakusa or Ueno?
faqs.answer: Ueno has more late dining and izakaya, especially around Ameyoko, while Asakusa is quieter after the temples close in the evening. For nightlife you would still go to Shinjuku or Shibuya, but between these two, Ueno stays livelier later.

stationRouteNote.title: Station & hotel-route note
stationRouteNote.commonBody: Tokyo station names can be confusing. The same name may refer to different railway operators, separate entrances, distant platforms, or nearby-but-not-identical station areas. This finder compares practical hotel-base areas, not exact station exits. Before booking, always check the walking route from your exact hotel to the exact station entrance or platform you plan to use.

areas.asakusa: Asakusa can refer to several different station locations. Tokyo Metro, Toei, Tobu Asakusa, and Tsukuba Express Asakusa are not all the same walking point. Check which station and line your hotel is actually closest to before booking.
areas.oshiage: Oshiage is useful for airport access and Tokyo Skytree, but the walking route can vary depending on whether your hotel is closer to Oshiage Station, Tokyo Skytree Station, or the Solamachi side.
areas.kuramae: Kuramae has separate Toei Asakusa Line and Oedo Line station locations. Check which side your hotel is closer to before booking, especially with luggage.
areas.kiyosumi-shirakawa: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa is a quiet local base, but hotel options can be limited and nearby areas such as Monzen-Nakacho or Koto may appear in hotel searches. Check the exact walking distance and nearest station before booking.
areas.roppongi: Roppongi can feel different depending on the line and exit. The Oedo Line platforms are deep, and some hotel routes involve slopes or longer walks than they look on the map.
areas.shinjuku: Shinjuku is a very large station area. West Exit, East Exit, South Exit, Shinjuku-sanchome, Nishi-Shinjuku, and Tochomae can feel like different hotel bases. Check the exact exit and walking route before booking.
areas.shibuya: Shibuya is compact on the map but can be confusing because of hills, exits, pedestrian decks, and underground routes. A hotel may look close to Shibuya Station but still require a less simple walk with luggage.
areas.tokyo-station: Tokyo Station can mean very different sides of the station. Marunouchi, Yaesu, Otemachi, and Nihombashi are not the same hotel location, especially with luggage or an early Shinkansen.
areas.ueno: Ueno is convenient, but JR Ueno, Tokyo Metro Ueno, Keisei Ueno, Ueno-hirokoji, and Ueno-okachimachi are not the same walking point. For Narita access, check whether your hotel is closer to JR/Metro Ueno or Keisei Ueno.
areas.ginza-yurakucho: Ginza / Yurakucho is a dense central area with several nearby stations. Ginza, Yurakucho, Higashi-Ginza, Ginza-itchome, and Shimbashi-side hotels can feel different for luggage and train access.
areas.hamamatsucho-daimon: Hamamatsucho and Daimon are close, but JR/Monorail Hamamatsucho and Toei Daimon are not exactly the same station entrance. Check which one your hotel is closer to, especially for Haneda access.
areas.shinagawa: Shinagawa is useful for the Shinkansen and Haneda access, but the Takanawa side and Konan side feel different. Check which side your hotel is on before booking.
areas.ikebukuro: Ikebukuro is a large station with very different east, west, north, and underground exit areas. A hotel can be "near Ikebukuro" but still be confusing to reach with luggage.
areas.akihabara: Akihabara can refer to JR Akihabara, Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line Akihabara, Tsukuba Express Akihabara, or the nearby Iwamotocho side. Check which station entrance your hotel is actually closest to.
areas.nihombashi: Nihombashi is central and practical, but hotel listings may overlap with Tokyo Station, Otemachi, Mitsukoshimae, Kayabacho, or Ningyocho. Check the exact nearest station rather than relying only on the area name.

methodology.title: How scores work
methodology.summary: Methodology, station-name notes, and source details are here if you want to inspect the scoring.
methodology.p1: This compares station areas, not individual hotels.
methodology.p2: Scores blend a 55% editorial travel-fit baseline, 35% station-usability adjustments (passenger crowd, exit / entrance complexity, line / operator count, transfer-hub penalty), and 10% live status. Each per-sub-score adjustment is capped at ±10.
methodology.p3: Step-free route confidence is read from Toei's public barrier-free CSVs where matched, and adds a small positive nudge to luggage-friendliness. Exit / entrance complexity is read from Tokyo Metro's public per-station exit feed where matched; areas without Tokyo Metro coverage use the editorial level. Missing data is never punished. Hotel choice density is editorial and does not evaluate individual hotels. Scores are not official ratings and do not rank safety, hotel quality, price, or availability.

meta.title: How to Read Japanese Train Signs: Line, Direction, Platform and Exit
meta.description: A practical guide for first-time visitors to Japan: how to read train signs, check the line, direction, platform, train type and station exit without getting confused.

hero.title: How to Read Japanese Train Signs
hero.body: First-time visitors often search for a destination station and then follow the first sign that looks close enough. In Japan, that is not enough. The calmer way to ride trains is to check the line, direction, train type, platform, and exit in order.

quick.title: Do not only check the station name.
quick.bodyRich: To ride trains in Japan, first check the station name, but never stop there. Confirm these five things before you board: <s1>line name</s1>, <s2>direction</s2>, <s3>train type</s3>, <s4>platform number</s4>, and <s5>exit or transfer gate</s5>. Japanese commuters do this almost automatically. Visitors usually need to slow it down and read the signs in that order.

why.title: Why station names are not enough
why.intro: A Japanese train trip is not a single question: “Which station am I going to?” It is a sequence of smaller confirmations. Large stations can contain several railway companies, multiple ticket gate areas, Shinkansen platforms, subway passages, shopping floors, bus terminals, and exits that point toward completely different neighborhoods.
why.p1: Tokyo Station is a good example. It is one station name, but it includes JR conventional lines, Tokaido Shinkansen, Tohoku and Joetsu Shinkansen, Marunouchi subway access, the Yaesu side, the Marunouchi side, underground shopping streets, bus terminals, and many exits. If your hotel is near the Yaesu South Exit, arriving at the Marunouchi side is not wrong, but it can add a long walk at the worst possible time: when you have luggage and are tired from a flight.
why.p2: Shinjuku Station is even more dramatic. It has JR, Odakyu, Keio, Tokyo Metro, Toei Subway, and huge underground passages. People say “meet at Shinjuku Station” as if it were one point on a map, but in practice Shinjuku is a district-sized transport machine. The same is true in a gentler way at Ueno Station, where JR Ueno, Keisei Ueno, Tokyo Metro Ueno, and the park side are related but not identical. Shin-Osaka is another case: it is both a subway station and a Shinkansen station, and the signs you follow depend on what kind of train you need next.
why.p3: Kyoto Station looks simpler on a map, but it still has Shinkansen gates, JR local and limited express platforms, subway access, bus terminals, and different hotel sides. Searching only for “Kyoto Station” is useful for reaching the station area. It is not enough for finding the right platform or the correct exit.
why.p4: The practical habit is to match your app instructions with real station signs. If your route says “JR Yamanote Line for Ueno,” find signs for JR first, then Yamanote Line, then the direction for Ueno. If your route says “Toei Asakusa Line Airport Express,” do not follow a Tokyo Metro sign only because the station name appears nearby. The station name is the destination. The line and direction are the path.

mental.title: The 5-step mental model
mental.intro: You do not need to understand the whole railway system. You only need a repeatable checklist for the next decision in front of you.

line.title: 1. Find the line
line.body: Start with the line name before you think about the destination. Large stations may contain JR lines, subway lines, private railway lines, and Shinkansen gates in the same complex. If your app says Tokyo Metro Ginza Line, do not follow a JR sign just because the destination station looks familiar.
line.examples: JR Yamanote Line · Tokyo Metro Ginza Line · Toei Asakusa Line · Tokaido Shinkansen

direction.title: 2. Check the direction
direction.body: Direction tells you which side of the line you need. Japanese signs often use major stations at the end of the route, not every small stop. If you are going only two stops, you still follow the direction shown by the larger destination printed on the platform sign.
direction.examples: for Shibuya · for Ueno · for Oshiage · for Kyoto · for Shin-Osaka

type.title: 3. Check the train type
type.body: Train type matters because trains on the same line and in the same direction may not stop at the same stations. A Local may stop everywhere. A Rapid or Express may skip smaller stations. A Limited Express or Airport Express may require a ticket or follow a different stopping pattern.
type.examples: Local · Rapid · Express · Limited Express · Airport Express · Shinkansen

platform.title: 4. Check the platform
platform.body: Use the platform number as confirmation, not as the only clue. Platforms can change, and one platform can serve different trains at different times. Match the line, direction, departure time, and train type before boarding.
platform.examples: Platform 3 · Track 14 · Shinkansen tracks · Same platform, different direction

exit.title: 5. Check the exit or transfer gate
exit.body: In Japan, the exit is part of the trip. The wrong exit at Tokyo Station, Shinjuku Station, or Kyoto Station can put you ten to twenty minutes away from your hotel or meeting point. Follow exit names, exit numbers, and transfer gate signs after you get off.
exit.examples: Yaesu South Exit · Hachiko Exit · A1 Exit · Shinkansen transfer gate

maps.title: How to use Google Maps with station signs
maps.intro: Google Maps is useful in Japan. It is especially useful for choosing a route, estimating travel time, seeing a platform number, and confirming a departure time. But inside Japanese stations, you still need to match the app with the real signs around you.
maps.p1: The most common mistake is trusting only the platform number. Platform numbers are helpful, but they are not a complete instruction. A platform can serve different lines, directions, or train types at different times. If your app says Platform 3, use that as a clue, then read the departure board above the platform. Confirm the line name, the direction, the train type, and the departure time. If those four things match, you can board with much more confidence.
maps.p2: Another important habit is to compare train type carefully. Google Maps may show “Rapid” because it is the fastest option. If you miss that Rapid train and the next train from the same platform is an Express, it may not be the same route. If you are unsure, wait a moment and check the destination display on the side of the train or the screen above the platform. You are not trying to memorize Japanese railways. You are simply matching the next sign.
maps.p3: Exits are also where apps and real signs need to work together. Google Maps may tell you to use an exit such as A1, Hachiko Exit, Yaesu South Exit, or Central Gate. Inside the station, signs may repeat the exit name many times, but only after you choose the correct passage. If the exit number disappears, stop and look for a general exit board. Many stations have maps near the ticket gates that show every exit and the nearby streets.
maps.p4: If you have already passed through the ticket gates and still feel lost in a deep underground passage, it is often completely fine to go up to street level and reset. This can be easier than trying to solve a maze of underground corridors while tired or carrying luggage. Once you are outside, you can use landmarks, street signs, and your map app again. At stations such as Shinjuku, Tokyo, Ueno, and Kyoto, going above ground may add a few minutes, but it can reduce confusion and help you understand which side of the station you are actually on.
maps.footnote: Maps and translation are much easier when your phone works before the first train ride. Keep your first route, hotel address, and station exit saved before you arrive.

confusing.title: Common confusing cases
confusing.intro: These situations confuse visitors, but they are manageable. You do not need to understand everything. Just check the next sign.

sameName.title: Same station name, different railway company
sameName.body: A station name can be shared by JR, Tokyo Metro, Toei Subway, Keisei, Keikyu, Odakyu, Tobu, Kintetsu, Hankyu, or another company. The stations may connect underground, but they can have separate ticket gates and different platforms. When an app says JR Ueno, Tokyo Metro Ueno, or Keisei Ueno, those details matter.

types.title: Local vs Rapid vs Express
types.body: Local trains usually stop at every station. Rapid and Express trains skip some stations. Limited Express trains may require a supplement, especially on private railways and airport routes. Do not assume faster is better. Faster is only better if the train stops where you need to get off.

skip.title: Trains that skip your station
skip.body: This is one of the most common mistakes for first-time visitors. You stand on the right platform, board a train going in the right direction, and still pass your station because the train type does not stop there. Check the stopping pattern on the platform screen or in your app before boarding.

transfer.title: Transfer gates
transfer.body: A transfer gate is not the same as a normal exit. At large stations, especially when moving between JR and Shinkansen or between railway companies, you may need to pass through a transfer gate that keeps you inside the paid area. If you accidentally exit the station, you may need to re-enter and possibly adjust your fare.

exits.title: Multiple exits
exits.body: Japanese stations can have many exits because they serve huge neighborhoods. Shinjuku has exits that feel like different towns. Tokyo Station has Marunouchi and Yaesu sides. Subway stations may use A1, A2, B3, or C exits. The exit can matter as much as the station.

shinkansenGate.title: Shinkansen gates inside large stations
shinkansenGate.body: Shinkansen gates are often inside or beside a larger JR station. At Tokyo Station, Kyoto Station, Shin-Osaka, and Ueno, do not stop after finding the station building. Continue following signs for Shinkansen, then check the correct transfer gate, ticket gate, platform, car number, and seat area.

airport.title: Airport trains and branching routes
airport.body: Airport trains can be confusing because one line may split, continue under another railway name, or have different services on the same tracks. Narita, Haneda, Asakusa, Oshiage, Shinagawa, Ueno, and Shinjuku can all appear in airport routes. Confirm the train name and destination before boarding.

reserved.title: Reserved seat and non-reserved seat confusion
reserved.body: On the Shinkansen and some Limited Express trains, reserved and non-reserved areas can be different cars. Your ticket may show a car number and seat, or it may allow non-reserved seating only. Before boarding, check the car number signs on the platform.

shinkansen.title: Shinkansen-specific note
shinkansen.intro: The Shinkansen is easier than many local stations once you understand the sequence, because long-distance trains have clear departure times, platform numbers, car numbers, and seat assignments. But it still has its own signs.
shinkansen.p1Rich: For the Shinkansen, check the <b1>train name</b1>, <b2>departure time</b2>, <b3>platform</b3>, <b4>car number</b4>, <b5>reserved seat</b5>, <b6>non-reserved seat area</b6>, and, if you are riding the Tokaido Shinkansen, the <b7>Mt. Fuji-side seat</b7>. Train names such as Nozomi, Hikari, and Kodama matter because they stop at different stations. Departure time matters because several trains can leave for similar destinations within a few minutes.
shinkansen.p2Rich: If you are buying a Tokyo to Kyoto ticket, read the <link1>Tokyo to Kyoto Shinkansen ticket guide</link1> before you book. If you are deciding whether a rail pass is worth it, compare <link2>JR Pass vs single tickets</link2>. For seat selection, start with the <link3>Mt. Fuji seat guide</link3>, then use the <link4>Shinkansen seat guide hub</link4>. If you are specifically riding west from Tokyo, the <link5>Tokyo to Kyoto Mt. Fuji seat guide</link5> explains what to watch for.

before.title: Before boarding checklist
before.intro: Run through this checklist before you step onto the train. It takes less than a minute and prevents most mistakes.

checklist.line: Am I on the right line?
checklist.direction: Am I going in the right direction?
checklist.stops: Does this train stop at my station?
checklist.exit: Do I know which exit or transfer gate to follow?
checklist.internet: Do I have internet for maps and translation?
checklist.shinkansen: If taking the Shinkansen, do I know my car number and seat?

practical.title: Try Station Practice before your first ride
practical.body: After learning how signs work, practice exits, transfer gates, platforms, and wrong-route recovery in a short station navigation mission.

priority.title: Priority seats (優先席)
priority.intro: One small thing to recognize on local trains before you start riding around Tokyo, Kyoto, or Osaka.
priority.p1: On local trains, you may see seats marked 優先席 (yūsen-seki), usually near the doors. These seats are intended for elderly passengers, pregnant women, people with disabilities, injured or sick passengers, and people traveling with small children.
priority.p2: As a visitor, the easiest rule is simple: avoid priority seats if you do not need them. If you sit there on a crowded train, be ready to stand immediately when someone who may need the seat boards.
priority.p3: Look for the priority seat stickers near the windows or above the seats. Some trains also use different seat fabric, straps, or floor markings around the priority seat area.

hard.q: Is it hard to use trains in Japan as a tourist?
hard.a: It is not hard if you follow the signs in order. The system is detailed, but the signs are usually consistent. Do not try to understand the whole station at once. Confirm the line, direction, train type, platform, and exit one step at a time.

maps.q: Should I follow Google Maps or station signs?
maps.a: Use both. Google Maps is useful for planning the route, platform, and departure time. Inside the station, match that information with the real signs because platforms, exits, and train types can still require confirmation.

types.q: What is the difference between Local, Rapid and Express trains?
types.a: Local trains usually stop at every station. Rapid and Express trains skip some stops. Limited Express trains may skip even more stops and may require an extra ticket. Always check whether the train stops at your destination.

stops.q: How do I know if a train stops at my station?
stops.a: Look at the stopping pattern in your transit app, the platform departure board, or the route map on the platform. If your station is not shown for that train type, wait for a Local or another service that does stop there.

shinkansen.q: What should I check before boarding the Shinkansen?
shinkansen.a: Check the train name, departure time, platform, car number, reserved or non-reserved seat area, and your seat. If you want to see Mt. Fuji on the Tokaido Shinkansen, also check the Fuji-side seat before booking.

exits.q: Why are there so many exits in Japanese stations?
exits.a: Large stations serve huge neighborhoods, department stores, underground malls, bus terminals, office towers, and railway companies. Exits are numbered or named so people can reach the correct side of the station without walking a long way above ground.

internet.q: Do I need internet to use trains in Japan?
internet.a: You can use trains without internet, but mobile data makes the first trip much easier. Maps, translation, platform updates, and exit guidance are much more comfortable when your phone works as soon as you land.

suicaGuide.title: How to Buy a Suica Card in Japan: Ticket Machine Steps + Simulator
suicaGuide.metaDescription: How to buy a Suica IC card in Japan: where to buy it, step-by-step ticket-machine instructions, Welcome Suica vs Suica, how much to charge first, and a free interactive simulator to practice before you land. Availability varies by station and date.
suicaGuide.intro: A Suica is a rechargeable IC card that lets you tap through train and subway gates and pay at buses, convenience stores, and many shops in Japan — no paper tickets, no fare math. This guide covers where to buy a Suica, how to buy one at a station ticket machine step by step, what you need before you start, the difference between a regular Suica and the tourist Welcome Suica, and how much to charge first. Quick answer: yes, tourists can buy a Suica in Japan, usually at ticket machines and staffed windows in major stations and at the airport train stations — though availability can vary by station and date. First, practice the whole ticket-machine flow on the free simulator below; then read the step-by-step buying guide, tips, and FAQ. A few minutes of prep makes your first real machine at the airport feel familiar.
